description: This figure presents S-N fatigue curves for Fe-Mn-Al-C alloy B specimens obtained from reverse bending fatigue tests. Solid black triangles and the corresponding solid fitted line represent specimen BL (longitudinal stress direction), while hollow white triangles and the corresponding dashed fitted line represent specimen BT (transverse stress direction). The x-axis records fatigue cycles ranging from 1×10⁵ to 2×10⁷, and the y-axis records applied stress in MPa ranging from 200 to 500. Both curves show that the bearable stress decreases as the number of fatigue cycles increases. Specimen BL consistently exhibits higher bearable stress than specimen BT at the same cycle number, confirming that the longitudinal BL specimen has better fatigue resistance than the transverse BT specimen. This result supports the core conclusion that the string-arranged ferrite phase in alloy B acts as a crack arrester when its orientation is perpendicular to the crack propagation direction in BL specimens, thus improving the overall fatigue performance of the material.

This is an optical micrograph of specimen BT, which is a transverse oriented Fe-29Mn-9.1Al-0.6C (alloy B) sample after 1050 °C solution treatment for 1.5 h followed by oil quenching, with a microstructure consisting of 90 vol.% austenite and 10 vol.% string-like arranged ferrite. A scale bar of 50 μm is provided at the bottom right. A clear fatigue crack propagating roughly perpendicular to the applied stress is observed in the image. Since the string arrangement of ferrite phase is parallel to the crack propagation direction, the crack propagates directly across the austenite grains without being impeded by ferrite. This figure confirms that the ferrite phase cannot act as a crack arrester when its alignment is parallel to the crack propagation direction, which explains the poorer fatigue performance of specimen BT compared to the longitudinal specimen BL of the same alloy, where ferrite alignment is perpendicular to crack propagation and provides crack arresting effect.

This is an optical micrograph of specimen BL, the longitudinal sample of Fe-29Mn-9.1Al-0.6C alloy (alloy B) after 1050 °C solution treatment for 1.5 h followed by oil quenching, with a 50 μm scale bar provided. The specimen microstructure comprises 90 vol% austenite matrix and 10 vol% string-like distributed ferrite phase. The image demonstrates that fatigue cracks, which initiate at slip bands only formed in austenite and propagate approximately perpendicular to the applied stress, are impeded by ferrite grains as the ferrite string arrangement is perpendicular to the crack propagation direction. This observation confirms that ferrite acts as a crack arrester in this orientation: the soft fine ferrite induces crack blunting at the initial fatigue stage and intense work hardening at the later stage to hinder crack propagation, explaining the superior fatigue performance of specimen BL over the transverse counterpart specimen BT.
